raw_response:
# SOC 2 Type II Readiness Assessment — CC1.1
## Crestwood Dynamics | Audit Period: January 1 – December 31, 2025

---

## Preliminary Scoping Note

Before findings, I want to document which evidence was evaluated and its relevance:

| Artifact | Relevant to CC1.1? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| ISGC Committee Meeting Log | Yes — Primary | Governance cadence, quorum, oversight activity |
| Security Metrics Dashboard | Yes — Supporting | Evidence of ongoing monitoring and oversight |
| Security Exception Register | Yes — Supporting | Evidence of structured risk governance and ethical accountability |
| ISGC Charter (GOV-ISGC-001) | Yes — Primary | Defines obligations against which performance is measured |
| Employee Satisfaction Survey | **No** | HR operational data; not material to CC1.1 security governance |

The employee satisfaction survey was reviewed and set aside. While it contains cultural indicators tangentially relevant to broader COSO integrity principles, it provides no direct evidence bearing on the committee's governance obligations and will not be cited in findings.

---

## Charter Compliance Baseline

Before findings, I document the charter's binding obligations to establish a clear evaluation baseline:

| Obligation | Source | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Quarterly in-person meetings | §4.1 | Third Wednesday of Jan, Apr, Jul, Oct |
| Quorum for official business | §3.2 | 3 of 5 voting members |
| Async review — advance notice | §3.3 | ≥ 5 business days before response deadline |
| Async review — written approvals | §3.3 | Written approval from quorum (≥ 3 members) |
| Async review — records retained | §3.3 | All materials and approvals documented |
| Async review — ratification | §3.3 | Ratified at the next in-person meeting |
| Minutes distribution | §6.2 | Within 5 business days |
| Standing agenda coverage | §5 | All six standing items per meeting |
| Board reporting | §6.1 | Quarterly summary from Chair to Board |

---

## Q2 Quorum Failure — Detailed Analysis

Before the formal findings, I document the Q2 situation fully because it is the most consequential event of the year.

The April 16 meeting proceeded with only 2 of 5 members (Richard Tanaka and Karen Petrova). The charter requires 3 of 5 for quorum. No decisions were made, which is appropriate and consistent with charter §3.2. However, the meeting log notes that action item AI-Q2-001 required the CISO to reschedule with full quorum — **and this rescheduling never occurred.** The Q3 async review then addressed its own Q3 agenda items, not the deferred Q2 business. No evidence exists that Q2 standing agenda items (risk register, policy pipeline, metrics, incident update) were ever formally completed with quorum. This is the single most significant control failure in the evidence set.

---

## Findings

---

### Finding 1: Q2 Meeting Lacked Quorum and Deferred Business Was Never Completed

**Short Title:** Q2 Quorum Failure — No Remediation

**Document Reference:** Committee Meeting Log, ISGC-2025-Q2; Charter §3.2, §3.3, §5

**Detailed Analysis:**

The Q2 meeting on April 16, 2025 was attended by only 2 of 5 members, falling below the 3-member quorum threshold required by charter §3.2. The meeting was appropriately demoted to an informational briefing with no decisions made. However, three compounding failures followed:

1. **Rescheduling never occurred.** Action item AI-Q2-001 explicitly directed the CISO to reschedule with full quorum. The meeting log states directly: *"rescheduled meeting never occurred."* No evidence of any remediation attempt — no special meeting notice, no async review invocation, no documentation of why rescheduling was abandoned.

2. **Q2 standing agenda items were left unresolved.** Per charter §5, each quarter requires review of all six standing items. The Q2 meeting covered only partial metrics and an incident update. Risk register review, policy pipeline, and compliance update were not completed with quorum authority for the entire Q2 period.

3. **The async procedure was not invoked.** Charter §3.3 provides an explicit mechanism for exactly this circumstance — extraordinary situations where a meeting cannot be held. The CISO used this mechanism successfully in Q3 but did not invoke it to address the failed Q2 meeting.

The result is a complete governance gap for Q2 2025: no quorum-authorized decisions, no risk register review, no policy pipeline review, and no compliance update covering the April–June period. This is particularly concerning because Q2 was also the quarter of the P1 ransomware attempt (INC-2025-062) and the highest incident volume of the year (44 total incidents, including the phishing campaign spike).

**Why This Is a Gap:** SOC 2 CC1.1 requires demonstrated, operating governance oversight. A quarter in which the oversight body failed to convene with authority and took no corrective action leaves a 90-day blind spot in documented governance — during the organization's most active threat quarter.

**Severity: High**

A Critical designation would apply if decisions were made without quorum (they were not) or if material risks went unacknowledged for the full year (Q3/Q4 partially compensated). High is warranted because the procedural failure was unmitigated, occurred during the highest-risk quarter, and was specifically identified in an action item that was then abandoned.

---

### Finding 2: Q2 Standing Agenda Items Were Only Partially Covered

**Short Title:** Incomplete Standing Agenda Coverage in Q2

**Document Reference:** Committee Meeting Log, ISGC-2025-Q2; Charter §5

**Detailed Analysis:**

This finding is related to but distinct from Finding 1. Even setting aside the quorum failure, the Q2 meeting covered only two of the six required standing agenda items: partial metrics review and an incident update. The following standing items received no coverage — even informally — in Q2:

- Approval of prior meeting minutes (Q1 minutes)
- Enterprise risk register review
- Policy review and approval pipeline
- Compliance update (beyond the single incident reference)
- Open items

The meeting log confirms this with the notation "agenda_items_covered: Metrics review (partial); Incident update." While the absence of quorum means no formal business could occur, the charter's standing agenda exists to ensure structured, periodic review regardless. There is no evidence that any of these items were addressed in any supplemental format during Q2.

**Why This Is a Gap:** Consistent coverage of standing agenda items is a charter obligation (§5) and is evidence that security governance was actively exercised throughout the period. Gaps in coverage reduce the defensibility of the claim that oversight was maintained continuously.

**Severity: Medium**

This is partially subsumed by Finding 1 but is noted separately because the incomplete agenda coverage is independently observable and auditable evidence. Auditors will look at agenda coverage across all four quarters as a pattern.

---

### Finding 3: Q3 Async Review — Charter Compliance Confirmed with One Notable Observation

**Short Title:** Q3 Async Review Substantially Compliant; VP Engineering Non-Response Warrants Documentation

**Document Reference:** Committee Meeting Log, ISGC-2025-Q3; Charter §3.3

**Detailed Analysis:**

I want to be precise here: **the Q3 async review substantially met the charter's §3.3 requirements and should not be treated as a gap equivalent in severity to the Q2 failure.** The following obligations were satisfied:

| §3.3 Requirement | Evidence | Met? |
|---|---|---|
| Extraordinary circumstance | CEO investor roadshow (documented) | Yes |
| CISO invokes procedure | "CISO invoked charter Section 3.3" | Yes |
| Materials distributed ≥ 5 business days in advance | Distributed July 9 for July 9–16 response window; charter requires 5 BD advance notice | See note below |
| Written approvals from quorum | CEO (Jul 14), CFO (Jul 15), GC (Jul 16), CISO (Jul 9) = 4 approvals | Yes (4 > 3 required) |
| Materials and approvals retained | "All materials and email approvals retained per charter requirements" | Yes |
| Ratification at next in-person meeting | Q4 meeting ratified Q3 async decisions | Yes |

**Advance Notice Observation:** The charter requires materials distributed "at least 5 business days in advance." Materials were distributed July 9. The last approval was received July 16. If July 9 is the distribution date and July 16 is the effective "meeting date," this represents 5 business days (July 10, 11, 14, 15, 16), which meets the requirement on a narrow reading. However, the CISO's own approval was submitted July 9 — the same day as distribution — which raises a question of whether the CISO reviewed materials independently or effectively pre-approved the package. This is an observation, not a clear gap.

**VP Engineering Non-Response:** Karen Petrova did not respond to the async review. The meeting log notes this but confirms quorum was achieved with 4 approvals. The charter requires quorum (3 of 5), which was met. However, there is no documentation of whether non-response was followed up, whether VP Engineering reviewed the materials, or whether her non-participation was noted and accepted. For future reviews, a brief documented outreach attempt to non-responding members would strengthen the record.

**Why This Is Noted (Not a Gap):** This is an observation and best-practice recommendation, not a control failure. The async procedure was properly invoked and substantively followed. Auditors should confirm their own reading of the 5-business-day timeline, but on the evidence available, the Q3 async review was compliant.

**Severity: Low (Observation)**

This is flagged as an observation rather than a finding. The control operated as designed. The VP Engineering non-response and the narrow advance-notice timeline warrant documentation but do not constitute control failures.

---

### Finding 4: Q2 Critical SLA Miss — 100% Threshold Not Met, Compensating Control Documentation Adequate

**Short Title:** Q3 Critical Vulnerability SLA Breach with Compensating Controls

**Document Reference:** Security Metrics Dashboard, KPI 2; Exception Register; Charter §5 (standing agenda: metrics review)

**Detailed Analysis:**

The metrics dashboard shows that Q3 2025 critical vulnerability remediation SLA compliance fell to 85%, below the implicit 100% target for critical (72-hour) vulnerabilities. The cause was CVE-2025-38921 in the payment module. The dashboard notes that compensating WAF rules were deployed within 4 hours, which is a meaningful compensating control.

This finding is relevant to CC1.1 because it tests whether the governance structure identified, escalated, and documented a material control deviation — which is itself evidence of operating integrity. The evidence shows:

- **The deviation was identified and documented** in the metrics dashboard (positive indicator)
- **A compensating control was deployed quickly** (4-hour WAF deployment) (positive indicator)
- **The committee reviewed this in Q3** (the async review included "incident retrospective for INC-2025-062" and "risk register update") (positive indicator)
- **Q4 returned to 100% critical SLA compliance** (positive indicator)

However, there is no documentation in the evidence provided of whether the CVE-2025-38921 SLA breach was formally escalated, whether a post-incident review was conducted for the patching delay specifically, or whether the patch timeline was formally accepted as a risk during the Q3 async review.

**Why This Is Noted:** For CC1.1, auditors look for evidence that exceptions to controls are surfaced through governance channels rather than quietly managed. The dashboard documents the miss, but the exception register does not show a corresponding entry for the patching delay itself (EXC-2025-007 relates to the legacy gateway CVE-2024-38812, a different issue). The governance trail for CVE-2025-38921 is incomplete.

**Severity: Low**

The control gap was temporary, compensating controls were deployed rapidly, and performance recovered in Q4. However, the absence of formal documentation in the exception register or a specific agenda item for this SLA breach represents a minor documentation gap.

---

### Finding 5: High-Risk Vendor Assessment Coverage Below Target with Incomplete Remediation Timeline

**Short Title:** Third-Party Risk Coverage Below Target — Two High-Risk Vendors Unassessed

**Document Reference:** Security Metrics Dashboard, KPI 5; Charter §2 (oversight of compliance and ethical practices)

**Detailed Analysis:**

KPI 5 shows high-risk vendor coverage at 92.9% against a 95% target. Two specific vendors — DataStream Analytics and NorthPoint Consulting — have outstanding assessment questionnaires as of November 2025. Follow-ups were sent in November 2025 per the dashboard, but no completion timeline or escalation path is documented.

For CC1.1, third-party risk assessment is directly relevant because it reflects the organization's commitment to ensuring that vendors who handle or access company data meet ethical and security standards. Gaps in vendor assessment leave unknown risks in the supply chain.

The following is not documented in the evidence provided:
- What data or systems these two vendors access
- Whether interim compensating controls are in place pending assessment completion
- Whether there is an escalation path if vendors continue to be non-responsive
- Whether the ISGC was specifically informed of these two named vendors at Q4

The Q4 meeting log includes "SOC 2 readiness update" as an agenda item and was attended by full membership, but does not specifically reference the vendor coverage gap resolution.

**Why This Is a Gap:** An outstanding assessment for two named high-risk vendors as of the November 2025 dashboard (prepared January 10, 2026) means the gap persisted through year-end. For a SOC 2 Type II audit covering through December 31, 2025, this represents a year-end open item without documented remediation or formal risk acceptance.

**Severity: Medium**

High-risk vendors represent meaningful supply-chain exposure. The gap is narrow (2 of 28 vendors) and partially mitigated by follow-up actions, but the absence of formal risk acceptance or escalation documentation is a control weakness.

---

### Finding 6: Board Reporting — No Evidence Provided

**Short Title:** Quarterly Board Reporting Not Evidenced

**Document Reference:** Charter §6.1

**Detailed Analysis:**

Charter §6.1 requires the Committee Chair (CEO Angela Morrison) to provide a quarterly summary to the full Board of Directors. This is a formal governance obligation that demonstrates upward accountability — a core element of CC1.1's requirement that commitment to integrity flows from the top of the organization.

No evidence of board reporting was provided in the evidence set. This could mean:
1. Board reports exist but were not included in the evidence package (most likely), or
2. Board reports were not produced

For audit purposes, absence of evidence is treated as a gap until the evidence is produced. An auditor cannot assert the control operated effectively without seeing the reports, the board meeting minutes referencing them, or equivalent documentation.

**Why This Is a Gap:** CC1.1 requires demonstrated commitment to integrity from leadership. Upward reporting to the Board is the mechanism by which ISGC activity is accountable to the highest governance tier. Without evidence of this reporting, the chain of governance accountability is incomplete.

**Severity: Medium**

This would escalate to High if it can be confirmed that reports were not produced (rather than simply not provided). At readiness assessment stage, this is a documentation gap requiring immediate remediation — the evidence needs to be located and included in the audit package.

---

### Finding 7: Security Awareness Training Below Target in Two Quarters — Systemic Root Cause Identified but Not Formally Addressed

**Short Title:** Recurring Training Completion Gap — No Formal Corrective Action Documented

**Document Reference:** Security Metrics Dashboard, KPI 3; Charter §5 (standing agenda: policy and compliance review)

**Detailed Analysis:**

Security awareness training completion fell below the 95% target in Q1 (93.2%) and Q3 (94.8%). The dashboard attributes both misses to "new hire onboarding delays in Engineering." This same root cause appearing in two separate quarters suggests a systemic issue rather than isolated incidents.

The full-year average of 95.4% satisfies the annual target in aggregate, but the quarterly misses indicate the control did not operate consistently throughout the period. For a SOC 2 Type II audit, **consistent operation** across the audit period is the standard — not just annual averages.

More significantly, there is no evidence in the provided documents that:
- A formal corrective action plan was developed to address the onboarding delay root cause
- The ISGC specifically reviewed this recurring miss and directed remediation
- The Engineering onboarding process was modified to prevent recurrence

The Q4 improvement to 97.3% is a positive indicator, but without documented corrective action, it is difficult to attribute this to deliberate remediation versus natural variation.

**Why This Is a Gap:** Recurring KPI misses with an identified root cause and no documented corrective action represent a weakness in the governance feedback loop. CC1.1 expects that the oversight function not only monitors metrics but directs improvement when those metrics fall short.

**Severity: Low**

The aggregate performance is near-target, Q4 showed strong improvement, and the gap is not large in magnitude. However, the absence of formal corrective action documentation on a recurring miss is a documentation weakness that auditors will note.

---

## Summary Table

| # | Finding Title | Document Reference | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Q2 Quorum Failure — No Remediation | Meeting Log ISGC-2025-Q2; Charter §3.2, §3.3 | **High** |
| 2 | Incomplete Standing Agenda Coverage in Q2 | Meeting Log ISGC-2025-Q2; Charter §5 | **Medium** |
| 3 | Q3 Async Review — VP Eng Non-Response & Narrow Notice Window | Meeting Log ISGC-2025-Q3; Charter §3.3 | **Low (Observation)** |
| 4 | Critical Vulnerability SLA Breach — Incomplete Governance Trail | Metrics Dashboard KPI 2; Exception Register | **Low** |
| 5 | High-Risk Vendor Coverage Below Target — Two Vendors Unassessed at Year-End | Metrics Dashboard KPI 5 | **Medium** |
| 6 | Quarterly Board Reporting Not Evidenced | Charter §6.1 | **Medium** |
| 7 | Recurring Training Completion Gap — No Formal Corrective Action | Metrics Dashboard KPI 3 | **Low** |

---

## Positive Indicators

In the interest of a balanced assessment, the following evidence supports CC1.1 operating effectiveness and should be documented in the audit file:

- **Q1 and Q4 meetings** were fully attended, quorum was met, all standing agenda items were covered, and minutes were distributed within the charter's 5-business-day window.
- **Q3 async review** was properly invoked under charter authority, achieved quorum, retained all records, and was formally ratified at Q4 — demonstrating mature use of the charter's alternative procedure.
- **Exception management** shows a structured, documented process: all exceptions have compensating controls, review dates, outcomes, and clear remediation paths. Three exceptions were fully remediated during the year.
- **Incident response** to INC-2025-062 (P1 ransomware attempt) was followed by enhanced controls (email filtering) that demonstrably reduced Q3/Q4 incident volumes — evidence that governance oversight drove operational improvement.
- **Security budget approval** ($3.2M in Q1; 2026 proposal in Q4) demonstrates executive-level financial commitment to the security program.

---

## Remediation Priorities Before Audit Fieldwork

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Produce evidence of all four quarterly Board reports per §6.1, or document that they were not produced and develop a corrective plan | CEO / GRC | Immediate |
| 2 | Document the formal closure of Q2 governance gap — acknowledge in writing that Q2 standing items were not completed with quorum and document any compensating review | CISO / GRC | Before fieldwork |
| 3 | Obtain written risk acceptance or assessment completion for DataStream Analytics and NorthPoint Consulting | CISO / GRC | Before year-end close |
| 4 | Document corrective action for recurring training completion miss in Engineering onboarding | CISO / HR | Q1 2026 |
| 5 | Establish policy for handling non-responses in async reviews | CISO | Before next async review |

---

*Assessment prepared for readiness purposes only. Findings reflect available evidence as provided. Auditor judgment on severity may adjust upon review of additional documentation during fieldwork.*
